title: Bahşiş Hesaplayıcısı — Kişi Başı Bahşiş ve Hesap Bölme
description: Bahşiş miktarını, toplam hesabı ve kişi başı payı hesaplayın. Özel bahşiş yüzdesi ve hesap bölme desteği.

faq.title: Frequently Asked Questions
faq.q1: How much should I tip at a restaurant?
faq.a1: In the United States, the standard restaurant tip is 18–20% of the pre-tax bill for adequate to good service. 15% is the historical minimum considered polite, though it is now seen as low by many service workers. 20% has become the de facto standard for satisfactory service at full-service restaurants, reflecting both gratitude and recognition that restaurant servers typically earn $2.13/hour in federal tipped minimum wage — well below standard minimum wage — with tips making up the majority of their income. 25% or more is appropriate for genuinely excellent service, accommodating special requests, or large groups. A quick mental calculation: move the decimal one place left to find 10%, then double it for 20%.
faq.q2: Should I tip on the pre-tax or post-tax amount?
faq.a2: Both are socially acceptable in the U.S., but tipping on the post-tax amount (which is the more common practice and the default in this calculator) results in a slightly larger tip. On an $85 bill with 8% sales tax: pre-tax tipping at 20% = $17.00. Post-tax tipping at 20% = $91.80 × 0.20 = $18.36. The difference is $1.36 — small enough that most diners choose post-tax for simplicity. Technically, the service rendered by your server has nothing to do with the sales tax collected on behalf of the government, so pre-tax is the more logically pure approach. In practice, the server appreciates either.
faq.q3: How do I split a bill with tip included fairly?
faq.a3: The simplest fair split for equal consumption: add the subtotal, tax, and tip together, then divide by the number of people. This calculator does that automatically. For unequal consumption, there are two approaches: (1) even split with tip pro-rated — calculate each person's food cost, then add their proportional share of tax and tip. (2) Individual itemization — each person pays their exact food/drink cost plus tax and tip on their portion. The even-split approach is far simpler and reduces social friction; use itemization only when consumption is very unequal (e.g., one person ordered alcohol and others did not).
faq.q4: What is the standard tip for different service types?
faq.a4: Tipping norms by service type in the U.S.: Full-service restaurant servers: 18–22%. Bartenders: $1–2 per drink or 15–20% of bar tab. Food delivery (app-based): 15–20%, minimum $3–5 regardless of order size. Taxi/rideshare (Uber, Lyft): 15–20%. Coffee shop/fast casual (tip screen): optional, $0.50–$1 on simple orders is courteous. Hotel housekeeping: $2–5 per night, left daily (not just at checkout). Valet parking: $2–5 on retrieval. Hair salon/barber: 15–20%. Spa services (massage, facial): 15–20%. Movers: $20–$50 per mover for a full-day move. Tattoo artists: 10–20% depending on session length.
faq.q5: Is it rude not to tip? What are the cultural differences?
faq.a5: In the United States, not tipping at full-service restaurants is generally considered disrespectful to the server, whose livelihood depends on tips due to the tipped minimum wage structure. Not tipping for poor service is acceptable, though leaving a small token tip (5–10%) communicates dissatisfaction more clearly than leaving nothing. International context: in Japan and South Korea, tipping is culturally inappropriate and may cause offense. In Australia and New Zealand, tipping is optional and modest. In most of Western Europe, rounding up or leaving a few euros is customary but a U.S.-style 20% tip is neither expected nor standard. In Canada, 15–18% is the norm, close to U.S. practice. Always research local tipping customs when traveling internationally.
faq.q6: How do I handle tipping when a service charge is already added?
faq.a6: Many restaurants add a mandatory service charge (often called 'gratuity' or 'service charge') for large parties — typically 18–20% for groups of 6 or more. If this fee appears on your bill, it replaces the tip — you are not expected to tip additionally, though you may do so for exceptional service. Always check your bill carefully: some establishments add a service charge for all tables, which is legal but must be disclosed on the menu or bill. If a service charge was already collected, tipping again effectively means paying 36–40% total gratuity — generous but usually unintended.
faq.q7: How do I tip fairly when sharing a bill with people who ordered very different amounts?
faq.a7: There are three common approaches for unequal bills: (1) Even split — everyone pays the same total regardless of what they ordered. Works best among close friends or when differences are small. (2) Pay what you ordered — each person pays for their own items plus their proportional share of any shared items, then each calculates their individual tip and tax. Most fair but requires more calculation. (3) The big spender covers the tip — the person who ordered the most pays a higher tip to compensate. A practical solution: use this calculator's even-split mode as a baseline, and let whoever ordered significantly less opt to pay slightly less by mutual agreement.
faq.q8: What is the most efficient way to split a bill at a restaurant without a calculator?
faq.a8: For quick mental math at a restaurant: (1) Calculate 10% of the bill (move decimal one left). (2) For 20%, double the 10% figure. (3) For 15%, take the 10% figure and add half of it. Example: $84 bill. 10% = $8.40. 20% = $16.80. 15% = $8.40 + $4.20 = $12.60. For splitting with tax: take the total on the receipt (already includes tax) and apply your tip percentage to that. Then divide tip + post-tax bill by number of people. This calculator eliminates all of this mental math and handles any bill, tip percentage, tax rate, and group size instantly.

content.whatIs:
Bahşiş hesaplayıcısı, bir çek birden fazla kişi arasında bölündüğünde bahşiş miktarını, bahşiş ve vergi dahil toplam hesabı ve kişi başı payı hesaplar. Fatura toplamını girin, bir bahşiş yüzdesi seçin, isteğe bağlı olarak yerel satış vergisi oranını ekleyin ve yemek yiyenlerin sayısını belirtin — hesaplayıcı zihinsel aritmetik olmadan ödeme yapmak için gereken her rakamı anında gösterir.

Bahşiş, çoğu insanın aldığı en sık finansal mikro kararlardan biridir. Restoran bahşişleri ve hizmet ödemeleri pek çok ülkede çalışanların gelirinin önemli bir bölümünü oluşturmaktadır. Grup yemeklerinde bahşiş hesaplamak — ne kadar yüzde verileceği, vergi öncesi mi sonrası mı verilmesi gerektiği ve eşitsiz tüketimle nasıl başa çıkılacağı konusundaki anlaşmazlıklar — sıkça yaşanan bir sürtüşme kaynağıdır. Bu hesaplayıcı tüm bu senaryoları masadaki herkesin doğrulayabileceği şeffaf adım adım aritmetikle çözer.

Restoranların ötesinde bahşiş pek çok hizmet etkileşimine uygulanır: teslimat sürücüleri, barmenler, otel temizlik görevlileri, kuaför hizmetleri, taksi ve araç kiralama sürücüleri, taşıyıcılar ve daha fazlası. Bu hesaplayıcının esnek bahşiş yüzdesi girişi her senaryoya uyum sağlar.
content.formula:
Adım 1 — Vergiyi faturaya uygula:
  Vergi Tutarı = Fatura × (Vergi% ÷ 100)
  Vergili Fatura = Fatura + Vergi Tutarı

Adım 2 — Bahşişi hesapla (vergi sonrası fatura üzerinden):
  Bahşiş Tutarı = Vergili Fatura × (Bahşiş% ÷ 100)

Adım 3 — Toplamları ve paylaşımı hesapla:
  Toplam Fatura = Vergili Fatura + Bahşiş Tutarı
  Kişi Başı Bahşiş = Bahşiş Tutarı ÷ Kişi Sayısı
  Kişi Başı Toplam = Toplam Fatura ÷ Kişi Sayısı

Tüm tutarlar 2 ondalık basamağa yuvarlanır.
content.example:
Örnek 1 — 4 kişilik standart akşam yemeği:
85,00 ₺ fatura, %8 vergi, %20 bahşiş, 4 kişi.
Vergi: 85,00 × 0,08 = 6,80 → Vergili fatura: 91,80
Bahşiş: 91,80 × 0,20 = 18,36
Toplam: 91,80 + 18,36 = 110,16
Her kişi öder: 110,16 ÷ 4 = 27,54

Örnek 2 — İş yemeği, yüksek bahşiş:
156,00 ₺ fatura, %9,5 vergi, %22 bahşiş, 3 kişi.
Vergi: 14,82 → Vergili: 170,82
Bahşiş: 170,82 × 0,22 = 37,58
Toplam: 208,40. Kişi başı: 69,47.

Örnek 3 — Bar sekmesi, yiyecek yok:
45,00 ₺ bar sekmesi, vergi yok, %20 bahşiş, 2 kişi.
Bahşiş: 9,00. Toplam: 54,00. Her biri: 27,00.

Örnek 4 — Hızlı %20 zihinsel hesap:
70 ₺ fatura → %10 = 7,00 → %20 = 14,00 bahşiş → 84 ₺ toplam.
content.howItWorks_1: Fatura toplamını girin — tahmindeki fiyatlar üzerinde vergi ve bahşiş öncesi tutar. Vergi fişte zaten dahilse (bazı ülkelerde standarttır), tam fiş tutarını girin ve vergi yüzdesini 0 olarak ayarlayın.
content.howItWorks_2: Hızlı ön ayar düğmelerini (%10, %15, %18, %20, %25) kullanarak veya özel bir yüzde yazarak bir bahşiş yüzdesi seçin. İsteğe bağlı olarak bahşişin vergi sonrası tutar üzerinden hesaplanması için yerel satış vergisi oranını ekleyin. Hesaplayıcı vergi tutarını ve vergili faturayı tam şeffaflık için ayrı ayrı gösterir.
content.howItWorks_3: Stepper kontrolünü kullanarak hesabı paylaşan kişi sayısını ayarlayın. Hesaplayıcı kişi başı bahşiş ve kişi başı toplam tutarı eşit bölüşüm için anında gösterir. Herhangi bir girişi değiştirdiğinizde tüm sonuçlar gerçek zamanlı olarak güncellenir. Aradaki verileri görmek veya masadaki diğerlerine aritmetiği açıklamak istiyorsanız döküm bölümünü kullanın.
content.whyItMatters:
Tipping is not merely a social convention — it is the primary income source for millions of service workers in the United States. The federal tipped minimum wage has been $2.13/hour since 1991, unchanged for over three decades. In states that have not set higher minimums (43 states still permit sub-minimum tipped wages), restaurant servers legally receive $2.13/hour in base wages from their employer, with the expectation that tips will bring total compensation above the regular minimum wage. When tips fall short, employers are technically required to make up the difference — but enforcement is inconsistent, and servers bear the uncertainty of variable income every shift.

Understanding this context changes how many people think about tipping. A 15% tip that felt generous in the 1980s — when it was established as the standard — is now considered minimal given three decades of inflation and stagnant tipped minimum wage. Many food service advocates and hospitality industry experts now recommend 20% as the baseline for satisfactory service, with 25%+ for excellent service or in recognition of the physical and emotional demands of the work. The few dollars between an 18% and a 22% tip are negligible to most diners; to a server working a 6-table section on a slow Tuesday, they represent a meaningful difference in a shift's earnings.

For group dining, the social dynamics of bill-splitting are a well-documented source of friction that affects both friendships and dining decisions. Research in behavioral economics shows that people who are uncertain about how to split a check fairly tend to underestimate their share (a cognitive bias called 'diffusion of responsibility') and that the person who initiates the calculation or suggests the amount sets a powerful anchor for the group. Using a transparent calculator that shows everyone's exact share eliminates this friction entirely — the math is visible, verifiable, and fair.
content.limitations:
This calculator performs an even split of the total bill (including tip and tax) among all listed people. It does not support unequal splits where different diners pay for different items or where some people did not consume alcohol. For group situations with significantly unequal consumption, you will need to calculate per-person totals before using the split function, or use a dedicated bill-splitting application that allows itemized entry.

The tax percentage field requires manual entry of your local sales tax rate. Restaurant sales tax rates vary by state and locality from 0% (Montana, New Hampshire, Oregon, Delaware, Alaska) to over 10% in some cities when combined with local surtaxes. If you are unsure of the local rate, check your receipt — the tax amount and rate are typically printed on U.S. restaurant receipts. Alternatively, skip the tax field if the bill amount you entered already includes tax.

Tipping customs vary dramatically by country, and this calculator is designed with U.S. tipping conventions as the default. In countries where service charges are included in menu prices (UK, Australia, most of Europe and Asia), tipping through a percentage calculator may result in double-tipping. Always verify local tipping expectations when dining internationally — tipping that is mandatory in the U.S. context may be optional, minimal, or in some cultures (Japan, South Korea) actually offensive.
content.tips_1: The fastest mental shortcut for any tip calculation: move the decimal one place left to find 10%, then scale from there. $73.50 bill → 10% = $7.35 → 15% = $11.03 (round up to $11) → 20% = $14.70 → 25% = $18.38. No phone needed for rough mental math. Use this calculator for precision when splitting with multiple people or paying card (where exact amounts matter).
content.tips_2: For consistent group dining, establish a simple group norm upfront: 'We always do 20% of the post-tax total, even split.' Having a pre-agreed standard removes the awkward negotiation at the end of every meal. Groups that establish tipping norms in advance tip more consistently and with less friction than those that decide per-occasion.
content.tips_3: When service is problematic — long wait times, incorrect orders, rude staff — consider tipping the kitchen-appropriate amount (15–18%) and speaking to the manager about the service issue. The kitchen staff (cooks, bussers) share tip-outs from servers in many restaurants and had nothing to do with service failures. Withholding the tip entirely punishes everyone in the restaurant, including those who served you well behind the scenes.
content.tips_4: For delivery tipping, err toward generosity regardless of order size. Delivery drivers are using their own vehicles, paying their own gas, and often working in unfavorable weather on tight time pressure. The base delivery fee paid to the app is largely retained by the platform, not the driver. A $4–5 minimum tip for any delivery, scaling to 15–20% for larger orders, reflects the true cost of the labor involved and makes a meaningful difference to workers earning near-minimum-wage hourly base rates.
